llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a within-subject design where participants wrote stories on Day 1 without AI and Day 2 with AI. The copilot arm represents the most collaborative human+AI condition and showed the highest quality scores among AI arms. Table S7 provides means for Day 1 (Without AI) and Day 2 (With AI) for the copilot group, along with standard errors. The same participants completed both conditions, making this a paired design.
llm_extracted.notes: Table S7 provides means and standard errors for overall quality on Day 1 (without AI) and Day 2 (with AI) for the copilot group. The constant row shows Day 1 mean=4.92 (SE=0.068) and Day 2 mean=5.0622 (SE=0.528) for the copilot condition. Total N=285 participants per Table 1, but 278 stories were produced on Day 1 per page 4. Using n=278 as the paired sample size. The design is within-subject (same participants, Day 1 vs Day 2). The SE for Day 2 appears unusually large compared to Day 1, which may reflect model uncertainty rather than pure sampling error. No SD of differences or paired t-statistic is reported. Python will need to convert SEs to SDs and apply paired-design correction.
source_quotes: Table S7. Overall Quality: Day 1 (Without AI): Constant 4.9200*** (0.068); Day 2 (With AI): Constant 5.0622*** (0.528); T3: Copilot -0.1518 (0.098) [Day 1], 0.3158*** (0.105) [Day 2]
A total of 549 stories were produced (278 from Day 1 and 271 from Day 2)

On Day 1, participants completed an initial survey... They then wrote a 1,000-word story without any AI assistance... On Day 2, participants wrote a new 1,000-word story... using ChatGPT 3.5, following their assigned collaboration model.

computed.notes: Table S7 provides means and standard errors for overall quality on Day 1 (without AI) and Day 2 (with AI) for the copilot group. The constant row shows Day 1 mean=4.92 (SE=0.068) and Day 2 mean=5.0622 (SE=0.528) for the copilot condition. Total N=285 participants per Table 1, but 278 stories were produced on Day 1 per page 4. Using n=278 as the paired sample size. The design is within-subject (same participants, Day 1 vs Day 2). The SE for Day 2 appears unusually large compared to Day 1, which may reflect model uncertainty rather than pure sampling error. No SD of differences or paired t-statistic is reported. Python will need to convert SEs to SDs and apply paired-design correction.
